Dear Ms. Mester:
This is to advise you that, upon consideration by the
Commission at its Septenber 5, 1990 meeting, the advice we gave to
you in the Mester Advice Letter, No. A-90-389, dated July 17,
1990, has been revised.
In your l-etter to the Commission, you sought advice as to
whether in itern received by each member of the California Public
Utilities 
Commission (the 'rcPuct') constituted 6 ttgiftrr, or, in the
alternative, fe11 under the "informational rnater.ials" exception to
the definition of gift under the Act. (section a2o2a. ) The itern
invol,ved r./as a scal-e rnodef of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant coll-ector plate. 
It had been given to the
CPUC commissioners by a smaIl power producer, Luz Development and
Finance corporation ("Luzrr), aLso known as Luz International
Lirnited. We inforned you that the item was a gift.
The Commission consiaered the appeal that hras filed by your
office on behaff of the cPUc conmission, and the oral and written
comments of interested persons who contended that the scale rnodel
was incorrectfy characterized as a gift. 
Based on the evidence in
the adrninistrative record before it, the Commission determined
that the scale model in question should not be deemed a gift to
each of the cPUc commissioners but, rather, should be classified
as rrinf ormati-ona I naterials'i which are not gifts under the Act.
(Section 82029 (b) (1) . )

Re: Reconsi,derat ion of Mester Advice Letter

Dear chairman Larson and Commissioners:
This firm represents Luz International Limited (rrLuzr).
Attached to this letter you will find a nemorandum regarding the
Iegal issues raised by the above-referenced matter. In this
letter, 
however, we hrould l-ike to focus on the poJ.icy concerns
raised by the advice letter -- policy concerns lrhich go !re1l
beyond the scope of inguiry made by the Staff of the Cornmission.
Ige are acutely aware of the legal issues raised by our
client's delivery of models of its solar collector to public
officials. 
We also understand and appreciate that the Commission
must adhere to precedent established through previous advice
letters and opinions, and must continue to develop its
interpretations of law in those important and comp]ex areas.
Nonetheless, we and our client are concerned that our
clientts rnistake and lack of sophistication in the Political
Reforn Act area has caused a delay in state energy policy
irnplementation. And, as you know, this will not merely irnpact
our Luzr issues, but the interests of other enerlIy producers, and
ultimately the public interest.
Our experience with the Political Reform Act and its
history lends us to conclude that the intent of the voters in
enacting the Political Reforn Act v/as not to hamstring government
in its essential operations. Our clientts mistake has already
cost the taxpayers significantly because of the untold hours of
tine spent by your staff and that of the Public Utilities
Conmission (r'cPuc"). our client regrets this and only wishes it
knel^, then what it knows now--nameIy, that it should have sent
five of the nodels to the cPUc itself.
we believe that at Ieast five neritorious
interpretations of the law exist which would allow your


ie
John E. Larson, Cha j.rman
Menbers of the Cornmission
August 31, 1990
Page 2
Commission to do justj,ce to its respons ibil ities to interpret the
statutes of the Political Reform Act in a sound but common sense
manner, and in a !/ay calculated to insure a just result, while
also insuring rninimun disruption of state business on crucial
energy issues. These opti,ons are discussed in greater detail in
the attachment. However, briefly stated, the options are as
f o 11or^rs :
L. 
The nodels provided to public officials 
can be
characterj,zed as inforrnational material provided to public
officiaLs to understand production from renevJable energy sources.
2. 
If the Comnission $/ere to characterize this model
as a gift, given the ambiguity on this issue, the Connission can
invoke the concept of constructive notice, and make the date of
the gift July 17 , L990, $/hen the Commiss j.on staff informed the
CPUC of its conclusion.
3. 
The nodel can be characterized as both
informational (the rnodel portion) and a gift (the name plate),
allowing for an apportionrnent of the gift anount.
4. 
Even if one were to conclude that the rnodel can be
characterized only as a gift, the fair market value of the gift
is clearly much less than $25O.OO
5. 
If the Commission concludes that the rnodel is a
January, 1990 gift valued above 9250.00, the Commission can find
that the rrruLe of necessityrr applies because of the anbiguity
over rrhether this vras indeed a gift at the time it was received.
We have every confidence that you, as Commission
menbers, will be able to interpretate the political 
Reforn Act in
a common sense and reasonabl-e fashion which protects, rather than
irnpairs, the broad pubLic interest at stake here.
we thank you for your consideration.
Very truly y

MEMORANDUM
August 30, L990
John H. Larson, chain[an
Fair Political Practices Conmi.ssion
Members of the Comnission
Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson, Parrinello & Muell-er
Mester Advice Letter Appeal
FROM:
RE3
This nemorandum is subrnitted on behalf of Luz
International Limited ('tLuzr'). A brief statement regarding the
background of the company and relevant additional facts, with
exhibits, is attached.
The california PubIic Utilities 
Cornmission ("cPUc") has
subrnitted an original and supplemental lega1 brief to you
regarding the issues raised. our focus is not to repeat but to
summarize. Based on the law applicable to the particular facts
and circunstances present here, we request that the Commissj-on
reconsi-der the advice rendered in Mester and direct the staff to
revise its advice accordingJ.y.
It is our position that there are a number of
neritorious interpretations of the taw in this area which would
effectuate the reLevant purposes of the Political Reform Act, but
which would not result in a virtual ban on the CPUC exercising
its necessary functions on matters relating to Luz. We feeL
most strongly that the models in question $/ere not gifts, both
because of our clientts intention in providing them, and because
of the infornation they provide about solar engineering.
Hos/ever, there are addj-tional arguments which we ask the
Comrnission to cons ider,
1. The nodel is not a qift, but informational material
provided to state public officiats 
whose job, in part, is to
understand the nechanics of energy production from other than
disposable resources. Thj.s would result in the rnodels falling
within the rrinformational naterialsrt exception to the definition
of gift in the Act (cov't Code S 82028(b)), and would permit the
CPUC comnissioners to participate in decisions affecting Luz and
others who were not associated with Luzt delivery of the models.


The advice in Mester was rendered r,/ithout your staff
having seen an actual model of Luz's Ls-3 collector. 
Luz e/i11
display one of these nodels at the September 5, 1990 meeting.
The rnodels are bulky and unsuitable as a paperweight, desk
ornament or r^/a11 decoration. (c.f. In re Nida (L977) 3 Fppc
Ops. 1.) 
The nodelsr only function is to demonstrate how Luzrs
most current solar energy generapion system vJorks. This is the
use for which Luz designed them.'
2. 
We acknowledge the possibility that the Commission,
for precedential reasons, may feel cornpelled to consider the
nodel a gift (as opposed to informationat material) , 
However,
given the appearance of the object and the facts and
circumstances present here, we think the Comnission can
appreciate that recipients could be honestly confused about this
designation, Therefore, it would be Iogical and equitable to all
effected parties to make the date of receipt of the qift
Julv l-7, 1990, v/hen the FPPC i.nformed the CPUC of its conclusion
reqardinq the character of the nodeLs, The 30 days allotted for
return of the rnodels prescribed in the statute would run frorn
that date. This determination wouJ.d permit the CPUC
commj.ssioners to participate, as an item is not a gift, 
and thus
not a basis for disqualification or reportable, if it is returned
within 30 days of receipt. 
(cov't Code S 82028(b).)
There is precedent for the Cornmission allowing
flexibitity 
in 'tdate of receiptrr in a situation where there was a
misunderstanding about a gift. 
(Duffy Advice, A-84-84. The CPUC
has described this letter in its supplemental materials. ) In
Duffv, the nisunderstanding was about who gave the gift. 
Here,
there was confusion over whether there was a gift or whether the
model was informational , 
(The staff indicates that only value
was unclear and does not view the model as ambiguous in
character. (Staff meno at p. 9.) 
However, staff also states
that the model does have "educationaL,t components (p. 7).)
A directive to staff to apply the doctrine of
constructive receipt (and thus permit tinely return) is a
solution that does not disturb existing precedents about what is
or is not a gift. 
Providing such flexibility 
regarding receipt
could v/ell be based on all facts and circumstances present in
this particular case.
A11 members of the CPUC returned the solar collector
rnodels to Luz within 30 days of being advised by FppC staff in
1 Luz understands that even if the models fall within the
"inforrnational rnaterials'r exception (and thus are not gifts), the
nodels nay possibly confer some personal benefit on the
recipients which will be reportable by Luz.


the Mester letter that the model lras a qift and did not faII
within the informational materials exceition.
3. If any portion of the model is a gift, 
we believe
that it is only the personalized nameplate.
The staff has stated in its mernorandun that it rrdoes
not dispute the fact that the model has an educational
component.rr (Staff nemorandum at p. 7.) 
The staff then
discusses why, in its view, ths ttgifltt conponent outweighs the
rrinforrnational materialsrr component, resulting in a gift. 
(Among
these reasons, the staff says the models were rpersonally given
to the CPUC commissioners during the winter holidays....tt 
In
actuality, however, the models were sent via UpS on
January 2, L990. They were sent then because the project using
the new solar technology dernonstrated by the model had begun
generating po$rer on December 28, 1989.)
If an iten has mixed characteristics, the political
Reform Act does not require that one component (gift or
j-nformational) predominate to the exclusion of the other. 
The
Consumers Union advice letter 
(Snvder Advice, No. A-85-131)
illustrates that an iten can be both infonnational and of
personal benefit. 
The solution in that letter was to hold the
lobbyist-enployer providing the free subscription to its magazine
accountable for the entire value of the subscription and the
recipient accountable for none of it. 
Holrever, an apportionment
could be made so that the recipient r^,ould be accountable for the
part whj,ch has no informational attributes. 
Here, the
personalized naneplate (vrith a value of 97.50) is not
infonnational.
4. If, despite the appearance of the nodel and its
inherent anbiguity of character, the Connission believes that
precedent requires you to consider the nodel exclusively a
"gift,tr then the nodel could be valued at fair narket vaIue.
Here, a reasonable estinate of the market value of the nodel
clearly is less than $250.
The Act reguires that any object which is reportable be
valued at 'rfair market value.rr (Gov't Code S 82025.5.) The
staff has stated in its memorandun that the vaLue of the LS-3
solar collector plate models is not at issue because Ms. Ilester
of the CPUC inforned the staff that the value lras more than $250.
Ms. Mesterrs infornation v/as based on Luzrs cost, which was rnore
than $250. However, the value to the recipient is the cost of
the iten only if it is so unique or unusual that there is no
market equivalent, 
(Regulation 18726. ) If similar items are
marketed, a public official 
may nake a good faith estimate of the
fair market value. (In re Corv (1975)1 FPPC Ops. 153.)
3


Solar models are avail-able on the rnarket. Luz wiII
bring a commercial,ly available model, and brochures regarding
such models, to the hearing. Luz paid more than market for its
models. Despite what Luz paid, the appearance of the models is
such that a good faith estimate of fair market value would not
approach the cost to Luz.
If the Comrnission finds that a good faith estimate of
the fair rnarket value of the LS-3 solar collector models is Iess
than $250, then the CPUC could perrnissibly participate in
decisions affecting Luz. The estimated fair market value still
would be reportable by the recipients on their economic j.nterest
statetnents (if the value is over 950, which it would be, based on
the commercial ly-ava i table eguivatents). Assurning the models
have some personal benef it, Luz r../ou td also report the modeLs on
its tobbyist-employer report, but at cost to j.t.
5. 
We beli.eve that even if the Cornmission were to find
the nodels to be a gift valued in excess of $250, there are sound
legal and policy reasons for the Commission concluding that the
rule of ttleqallv required participationrr applies $rhere the
condj.tions of covernment Code section 871-01 are met. Application
of the rufe !,/ould permit CPUC members, randomly selected, to
participate in the pending decisions affecting Luz (and others) .
Both Luz and CPUC nembers vrould report the models, including the
cornmissioners chosen to partic j.pate,
The facts in the Hopkins opinion can be distinguished
from the facts here, Ilopkins only prohibited application of the
rule of legaIly required participation to those gifts that were
clearly gifts at the time of receipt and which were for the
enj oynent of the public official-. 
UnIike the Disneytand tickets
or wine discussed in Hopkins, Luzrs models have no entertainment
vaLue and are not consunable.
Most important, in Hopkins, the donor (Disneyland) was
not involved in a conplicated regulatory natter affecting
numerous other parties also Iikely to be affected. There, if the
Anaheim City Council could not vote on matters foreseeably and
naterially affecting Disneyland, no one but Disneyland would
suffer if the rule of necessity permitting the city to continue
to conduct business did not appIy. Here, that is not the case.
Although the staff states that rr...the Act does not designate
eguitable grounds, such as the doctrine of fairness, as a basis
for non-application of the Hopkins opiniontr, Luz bel,ieves the
Commission rnay consider issues of pol j,cy and equity vithout
express statutory authority.
erhaps Luz may not be deserving of equity in f.ight of
the fact that it caused this problem. Luz now reallzes th;t the
potential conflict of interest and disqual i fication issues raised
by the Mester Advice letter could have been avoided had Luz sent


the nodels to the CPUC itself rather than to its nenbers. (This
mechani.sm has apparently been ernployed to gi-ve iterns to the state
Assembly for display in legislators' offices. Milman Advice, I-
90-337.) However, at the time the models of its solar collector
plate lrere sent, Luz was not familiar with this method.
The penalty the Mester Advice Letter irnposes on Luz for
an inadvertent error is extremely severe, as a company of Luzts
size cannot easily bear months of construction delays. penal,ties
will also attach to others not responsible for Luzrs conduct. We
therefore request your reconsideration of the Mester Advice
letter.

BICXGROUI{D AIID CERONOIOOY
(wttb Bxhtbits)
Luz International Linited (col1ective1y. rLILn). a
solar enerqy producer, is the parents collpany of five Luz
subsidiaries. Each subsidiary has its ovn chief executive and
staff. 
A1l.have a great deal of nanagenent autonony. Within
each subsidiary, Luz enploys an entrepreneuri.al nanlgement styl.e
lrhich encourages independent dec is ion-making. this managemenl
structure is necessary because Luz nust annually design, obtain
pernits for, .develop, finance, construct and op-rate i large
(80 uW) utility-scale solar energy generating systen (SEGSt.
The progran to distribute nodeLs of its state-of-the-
art solar collector originated in Luz's subsidiary, Luz Finance
and Developlrent Corporation (LPD).
The nodels at issue in Mester were built to scale and
were constructed to nove so that the solar technology could be
denonstrated. Each hodel sent to CPUC nerbers and other
recipients llas accotnpanied by a color diagran shorring ho!, the
parabolic solar collector h,as integrated into Luzrs power
generating systen, An original such diagran is attaahed as
Exhibit A.
The tnodels demonstrated for the first tiue the third
generation (LS-3) of solar technology used by Luz. The uodels
lrere sent after the start-up on Decenber 28th of the SEGS VIII
project, the eighth Luz solar project. 
SEGS VIII r\'as the first
project where the LS-3 technology was used for the entire solar
fj.eld, and the first 80 MW solar project. The January 2nd
distribution (rrhich vas after the hol.idays) i,ras intended to
soincide uith this event,
On the average, the solar collector nodels cost Luz
Finance and Developnent Corporation $306 each, trithout the
naueplate and the Luz pronotional plate and !'ithout shipping
charges, The value of the personalized naneplate was giiSO]
The total number of Ls-3 nodels Luz Finance distributed
vras 127. A list is attached as Exhibit B. The recipients
included people in other Luz subsidiaries, participants in l,uz
projects, Luzrs outside legal counsel and oihers with an interest
in soLar technology. Relatively feu e.ere p"Uii- "iii"i.f..
The nodels lrere distributed lrlth a forn letter uhich
did not vary except for the addressee and salutati;n. e copy ot
a sanple letter is attached as Exhibit C.
The nodels for th6 CPUC connissioners rrere sent UpS,
second day delivery guaranteed, on January 2, lgg|.

Dear IiIr. HalLabrin:
I enclose herewith the california Public Utilities 
commission's
(cPUc's) appeal to the Fair Political Practices Cornmission's
(FPPc's) Advice Letter No. A-90-389, dated July L7, 1990. That
letter concluded that models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric polrer plant collector plate, which were provided to each
nember of the CPUC, are gifts and do not faII under the
'informati,onal materials" exception to the definition of gift
under the Act. The Advice Letter also pointed out in footnote 7,
page 6, that because gifts were involved, the 'rule of
necessity,' codified at Government code section 87101., could not
be applied to the facts presented by the CPUC. (In re Hopkins, 3
FPPC ops. 1,07. )
The cPuc's primary arg"ument is that the nodels have been
incorrectly characterized as gifts. 
rurthermore, even if the
FPPC continues to categorize the models as gifts, the FPPC should
1et the cPUc cure the problen by letting the Comrnissioners return
the models irurediately. In additj.on, the FPPC should find that
the holding of the Hopkins opinion on the 'rul-e of necessity"
should not be applied to this set of facts.
The cPUc requests that its appeal be placed on the agenda of the
Fair Political Practices Cornrnission (FPPC) meeting of August 7,
1990, and that the cPUc be given an opportunity to make a
presentation to the FPPC at that tine. 
As explained in the
attached appeal , because of the severe time constraints
which govern the proceeding currently before the CPUC which is
directly affected by the FPPC'S Advice Letter, the CPUC urges the
FPPC to act on this appeal, if at all possible, on August 7.
Sincerely,

JANICE E. KERR

ANNE K. MESTER
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Franc j.sco, CA 94LOz
(4r,5) 557-2 3 8 0
Attorneys for the zub1ic
Utilities 
Commission of
the State of Cal-ifornia


lNTRODUCTION
The california Public Utilities comnission (CPUC) hereby
appeals the Faj-r Political Practices Commission's (FPPC's) Advice
Letter No. A-9o-349, issued July 17, 1990. That Advice Letter
concludes that 6ca1e models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric power plant collector plate, which l^rere provided to each
nenber of the CPUC by the Luz Development and Finance corporation
(tuz), are gifts and do not fatt under the 'informational
naterials' exception to the definition of'gift'under 
the
Political Reforn Act (Act). The Advice L,etter also points out in
a footnote that because gifts are involved. the 'rul-e of
necessity,' codified at Government code section 87101, cannot be
apptied to the facts presented by the cPUc. (In re Hopking, 3
FPPC Ops. 107. )
For the reasons set forth belor,r, the cPUc contends that the
nodels have been incorrectly characterized as gifts, and that
even if it is assuned that they are gifts, the HgpLLlq
holding on the orule of necessity' should not be applied to this
set of facts.
The cPUc requests that the FPPC find that the nodels are not
gifts. 
rf the fPPc continues to categorize the models as gifts,
the CPUC requests that the FPPC allov the CPUC to cure the
problern by fetting the conmj.ssioners return the models
iNrediately. fn addition, the cPUc requests the FPPC to find

I. 
The Solar Models Are Not Gifts.
The Advice Letter concludes that the models, which were

utilizing state of the art technology to produce solar
electricity.

electricity.

Luz system, as exenplified by the model , operated. They offered
our commissioners not onl-y by the model , operated. They of
the opportuni,ty to learn about t
e it is a major component of the
n industrv. is directlv related
rtuni,ty to learn about the
technology -- which, because it
independent power production industry,
directly related to the
r-nctepenclent povrer procluctron lndusEry, rs d].recEl"y reLaEed Eo Ene

It came as a total surprise when the Commissioners Iearned, well
after 30 days fron receipt, that the 'cost' to Luz was reported

Even thouqh Luz has reported its cost to be over $300, the
value to each Comrnissj-oner is practically nothing. In re Corv, 1
FPPC Ops. 153, held that in terms of valuation of unique gifts,
it is not necessary to have an appraiser assess the value of the
item; all the FPPC will require is a good faith effort on the
part of agency officials to place a val-ue on an item rdhose fair
market value cannot be easily ascertained. Even had there been
some arnbiguity in the cPUc commissioners, minds over the status
of the models, they in good faith believed then to have a value
of well- below $250. The models cannot be resold for anything
more than a very minor amount; they are not of the nature of art


objectsi they are not like season tickets or free passes (whether
or not those items are valued in terms of their rnaximum potential
use or in terms of each actual use) i moreover, the quality of

Even had the Commissioners considered the models to be

Conrnissioners had no qood reason to believe their value exceeded
$2s0.
This does not nean that the models do not have some

unfair to use the cost to the donor as the sole figure for

certainLy be considered to be weII bel-ow the $250 linit 
for
disqual if ication purposes.
The CPuc remains of the view that the nodels fall into the
'inforrnational materialso exception to the definition of 'gift.'
The notion of 'gift," 
as defined in the Act, is that something is
given to someone without any reci.procal consideration flowing
back to the donor. But this is true of informational- rnateri.als
as well as any other tangrible or intangible iten. 
The apparent
key to the distinction, as discussed by the FPPC at page 4 of the
Advice Letter, is that no personal benefit accrues to an
individual recipient frorn receipt of an informational naterial;
rather, the benefit flows to the agency itself 
(as, for exanple,
with the tours which the FPPC has found to be informational).
Under this standard, the nodels clearly fit under the

irnportant solar technology, which, as part of the
sma1l power production industry in California, is directly
related to the CPUC's regulatory respons ibil ities . This

what little

In light of the above facts, it is nanifestly unfair to

such an application could hre11 place

In re Hopkins, 3 FPPC ops. L07, on a 3-2 vote, holds in
pertinent part that the statutory ruLe of necessity, Govt. code
Section 87101-, [1] nay not be invoked to aIlo$, a disqualified
official to vote on a decision if the basis of that
disgualification is a gift from a donor financially interested in

same ent j.ty, and if it could be reasonabl-y foreseen at the time
the gift was received that the official 
wouLd be asked to make or
participate in making a governmental decision affecting the
donor.
Section 871o1 provides :
'section 87L00 [the basic disqua]. i fication sectj.onl does
not prevent
participati
any public official

g in the making of a governmental decision to
for the

The FPPC has enacted a regTulation implernenting Section 87101
which states, in pertinent part:

is not leqallv recruired to make or to

of Governnent Code Section 87101 unless

The cPuc contends that application of the rule set forth in
Hopkins to the instant facts will lead to the 'mischief'
predicted by the two dissenters to that opinion, and that
even if the FPPC continues to categorize the solar rnodels as
gifts, the Hopkins holding on the rule should not be applied to
the facts of this case.
In Hopkins, the iterns involved were never seriously
considered to be anything but giftst the prj.rnary question was how
they should be valued. Here, the cPUc never seriously considered
the nodels to be giftsi 
from the date of their receipt, our
Commissioners, in good faith, viewed them as working models of a
technology which was relevant to the cPuc's regulatory
responsibil ities, of an educational/ informati.onal nature, and of
a val.ue considerably less than $250. while it rnay have been
reasonably foreseeable at the time of receipt of the nodels that
a decision affecting Luz could be before the CPUC within the
year, it was never reasonably foreseeable that the rnodels would
be viewed as prohibited gifts.
Secondty, the Hopkins opi.nion was applied prospectivefy, the
FPPC recognized that the councilmembers requesting the advice in
that case night hrel.l have received tickets and passes which,
under the standard of valuation used in that opinion, would
subject them to disgual ification. 
Since they could not return
thern within the 30 days reguired by the Act, applying the opinion
retroactively would be unfaj,r and would 'unduly harnper the
operations of the city council.' 
Hopkins, 3 FPPC ops. at Ll-7.
Thus the opinion was only applied prospectively, and not to any
passes or tickets already received which were returned promptly
to their donors. Fairness requires the sane result here. The
CPUC commissj-oners, in good faith, believed the models to be
educational, and not gifts. 
They a1so, in good faith, believed
then to be of nominal personaL value. However, out of an
abundance of caution, as soon as they became aware of Luz,
valuation figure, they sought advice from the FPPC. In the face
of these facts, to now apply a finding that the models are gifts
which subj ect the CPUC Comnissioners to disqual ification, with no
opportunity to return thern, would be manifestly unfair.
Fina11y, the Hopkins opinion occurred in the context of the
ordinary business of a city council . There were no unusual
circumstances presented which required the FPPC to consider how
the concept of 'l-egall,y required participation, might be
construed under those circumstances. In the instant case,
unusual circumstances exist. 
The decision before the CPUC i.s not
just ordinary day-to-day business. This decision is governed by


other statutes which establish a deadline by which, if the
Commission does not act, the proposed proj ect is deemed approved
by operation of lahr. No other agency can decide the issue. The
cPuc maintains that if any case presents a situation where an
official 
is 'IegaIIy required' to participate in making a

III. 
The FPPC should consider overturninq The Hopkins opinion.
The Hopkins rnaj ority begins by citing case Law describing
the analogous connon lau, rule of necessity: 'Under that rule,
even if an official 
is othervise disgualified, the official 
nay
still 
act if there is no alternative source of decision and i.f
his failure to act would necessarily result in a failure of
justice. [cites. ]' 
Honki,ns, 3 FPPC Ops. at 115. The najority
then takes it upon itself to legislate a new meaningT for this
rule which has no basis in the case 1aw, in the statutory
analogue, or anywhere e1se.
It is well established that adrninistrative agencies must
respect existing statutes and case law in rulenaking. The FPPC,
for example, must adopt rules and regulatj.ons in accordance with
the Administrative Procedure Acti those rules and regulations
must be consistent with the Political Reform Act and other
applicable law. Section 83112. The Hopkins opinion utterly
fails to satisfy this requirement. Section 82030(a) explicitly
includes gifts as part of income. There is nothing in that
section, in Section 871,0L, in any other provision of the Act (as
it presently exists or as it existed in l-977), or, for that
matter, in any case law discussing the conmon 1a$, rule of
necessity, which justifies treating gifts differently from other
sources of income for purposes of invoking the statutory rule of
necessity. Hovrever, providing no justification, 
the FPPC does
just that: 
it decides that the rule of necessity neans one thing
when gifts are involved, and another when other kinds of
interests are involved, namely, income and/or investnents.
In addition to being an unlawful attenpt to legislate,
Hopkins is also ilIogicaI . The najority clains that including
gifts within operation of the rule would 'condone or even
encourage circumvention of the Act's conflict of interest
provisions.' 
Ilopkins, 3 FPPC Ops. at 10. It is. difficult 
to see
how inclusion of incorne and investrnents within operation of the
rule avoids this evil, lrhereas inclusion of gifts, especially
under the facts of this case, would foster it.
rinally, the Hopkins interpretation of Section 87L01- relies
upon a strai.ned reading of the phrase 'legally required
participation.' 
The najority appears to say that public
officials 
can claim they are Iegally required to participate in
any decisj-on, regardless of a conflict of interest, unl,ess that
confl.ict j-nvolves a gift. 
This expl-icitly includes day-to-day
operation of the agency's business. It cannot be the intent of
the Pol,itical Reform Act that public officials with such blatant


conflicts as receiving incoue or having investnents in conpanies
which come before then for governnental decisions can continue to
act under a rationale of 'tegally required participation,'
rrhereas officials $ho have received gifts fron those sane
companies cannot.
The 'nischief' which the Hopkins dissenters clained was
created by that deciEion has indeed occurred here, The cPUc
connissioners received an iten vhich they believed to be exenpt
from the Act, and thus did not return it uithin 30 days. Because
its value, as presentty determined, exceeds $250, those
conmissioners are nov autonatically disquatified from taking any
action involving the donor. In this case, the action not taken
wouLd have been in furtherance of the public interest: a project
lrould have been approved under the California Environmental
Quality Act, lrith appropriate nitigation neasures Largely
designed to protect public safety. If the project is deemed
approved by operation of tav vithout explicit CPUC approval, it
is unclear vhat the fate of those nitigation neasures might be.
lroreover, any delay in resolution of the issue of approval rdiLl
leave the active parties in this case, including the applicant,
the southern california Edison conpany, in a tenuous legal.
posture and uncertain about their financiaL and other
responsibilities for the project.
8


CONCLUSION
For all of the above reasons, the cPuc urges the FPPC to
find that the solar models are not gifts. 
If the FPPC declines
to make this finding, the CPUC requests it to allow the
Cornmissj.oners to cure the problem by returning the nodels
innediately. In addition, the CPUC requests the FPPC to find
that the holding in Hopkins on the 'rule of necessity' is not
appLicable to the facts of this case.

The california PubIic Utilities 
Cornmission ("cPUc") has
subrnitted an original and supplemental lega1 brief to you
regarding the issues raised. our focus is not to repeat but to
summarize. Based on the law applicable to the particular facts
and circunstances present here, we request that the Commissj-on
reconsi-der the advice rendered in Mester and direct the staff to
revise its advice accordingJ.y.
It is our position that there are a number of
neritorious interpretations of the taw in this area which would

which would not result in a virtual ban on the CPUC exercising
its necessary functions on matters relating to Luz. We feeL

Hos/ever, there are addj-tional arguments which we ask the
Comrnission to cons ider,
1. The nodel is not a qift, but informational material
provided to state public officiats 
whose job, in part, is to
understand the nechanics of energy production from other than
disposable resources. Thj.s would result in the rnodels falling
within the rrinformational naterialsrt exception to the definition
of gift in the Act (cov't Code S 82028(b)), and would permit the
CPUC comnissioners to participate in decisions affecting Luz and
others who were not associated with Luzt delivery of the models.


The advice in Mester was rendered r,/ithout your staff
having seen an actual model of Luz's Ls-3 collector. 
Luz e/i11
display one of these nodels at the September 5, 1990 meeting.
The rnodels are bulky and unsuitable as a paperweight, desk
ornament or r^/a11 decoration. (c.f. In re Nida (L977) 3 Fppc
Ops. 1.) 
The nodelsr only function is to demonstrate how Luzrs
most current solar energy generapion system vJorks. This is the
use for which Luz designed them.'
2. 
We acknowledge the possibility that the Commission,
for precedential reasons, may feel cornpelled to consider the
nodel a gift (as opposed to informationat material) , 
However,
given the appearance of the object and the facts and
circumstances present here, we think the Comnission can
appreciate that recipients could be honestly confused about this
designation, Therefore, it would be Iogical and equitable to all
effected parties to make the date of receipt of the qift
Julv l-7, 1990, v/hen the FPPC i.nformed the CPUC of its conclusion
reqardinq the character of the nodeLs, The 30 days allotted for
return of the rnodels prescribed in the statute would run frorn
that date. This determination wouJ.d permit the CPUC
commj.ssioners to participate, as an item is not a gift, 
and thus
not a basis for disqualification or reportable, if it is returned
within 30 days of receipt. 
(cov't Code S 82028(b).)
There is precedent for the Cornmission allowing
flexibitity 
in 'tdate of receiptrr in a situation where there was a
misunderstanding about a gift. 
(Duffy Advice, A-84-84. The CPUC
has described this letter in its supplemental materials. ) In
Duffv, the nisunderstanding was about who gave the gift. 
Here,
there was confusion over whether there was a gift or whether the
model was informational , 
(The staff indicates that only value
was unclear and does not view the model as ambiguous in
character. (Staff meno at p. 9.) 
However, staff also states
that the model does have "educationaL,t components (p. 7).)
A directive to staff to apply the doctrine of
constructive receipt (and thus permit tinely return) is a
solution that does not disturb existing precedents about what is
or is not a gift. 
Providing such flexibility 
regarding receipt
could v/ell be based on all facts and circumstances present in
this particular case.
A11 members of the CPUC returned the solar collector
rnodels to Luz within 30 days of being advised by FppC staff in
1 Luz understands that even if the models fall within the
"inforrnational rnaterials'r exception (and thus are not gifts), the
nodels nay possibly confer some personal benefit on the
recipients which will be reportable by Luz.


the Mester letter that the model lras a qift and did not faII
within the informational materials exceition.
3. If any portion of the model is a gift, 
we believe
that it is only the personalized nameplate.
The staff has stated in its mernorandun that it rrdoes
not dispute the fact that the model has an educational
component.rr (Staff nemorandum at p. 7.) 
The staff then
discusses why, in its view, ths ttgifltt conponent outweighs the
rrinforrnational materialsrr component, resulting in a gift. 
(Among

In

If an iten has mixed characteristics, the political
Reform Act does not require that one component (gift or

The
Consumers Union advice letter 
(Snvder Advice, No. A-85-131)
illustrates that an iten can be both infonnational and of

The solution in that letter was to hold the

Holrever, an apportionment
could be made so that the recipient r^,ould be accountable for the

Here, the
personalized naneplate (vrith a value of 97.50) is not

4. If, despite the appearance of the nodel and its

solar collector plate models is not at issue because Ms. Ilester
of the CPUC inforned the staff that the value lras more than $250.
Ms. Mesterrs infornation v/as based on Luzrs cost, which was rnore
than $250. However, the value to the recipient is the cost of

market equivalent, 
(Regulation 18726. ) If similar items are
marketed, a public official

3


Solar models are avail-able on the rnarket. Luz wiII

If the Comrnission finds that a good faith estimate of

5. 
We beli.eve that even if the Cornmission were to find

legal and policy reasons for the Commission concluding that the

condj.tions of covernment Code section 871-01 are met. Application
of the rufe !,/ould permit CPUC members, randomly selected, to
participate in the pending decisions affecting Luz (and others) .
Both Luz and CPUC nembers vrould report the models, including the
cornmissioners chosen to partic j.pate,
The facts in the Hopkins opinion can be distinguished
from the facts here, Ilopkins only prohibited application of the
rule of legaIly required participation to those gifts that were
clearly gifts at the time of receipt and which were for the
enj oynent of the public official-. 
UnIike the Disneytand tickets
or wine discussed in Hopkins, Luzrs models have no entertainment
vaLue and are not consunable.
Most important, in Hopkins, the donor (Disneyland) was
not involved in a conplicated regulatory natter affecting
numerous other parties also Iikely to be affected. There, if the
Anaheim City Council could not vote on matters foreseeably and
naterially affecting Disneyland, no one but Disneyland would

Although the staff states that rr...the Act does not designate
eguitable grounds, such as the doctrine of fairness, as a basis
for non-application of the Hopkins opiniontr, Luz bel,ieves the
Commission rnay consider issues of pol j,cy and equity vithout
express statutory authority.
erhaps Luz may not be deserving of equity in f.ight of
the fact that it caused this problem. Luz now reallzes th;t the
potential conflict of interest and disqual i fication issues raised
by the Mester Advice letter could have been avoided had Luz sent


the nodels to the CPUC itself rather than to its nenbers. (This
mechani.sm has apparently been ernployed to gi-ve iterns to the state
Assembly for display in legislators' offices. Milman Advice, I-
90-337.) However, at the time the models of its solar collector

The penalty the Mester Advice Letter irnposes on Luz for

will also attach to others not responsible for Luzrs conduct. We
therefore request your reconsideration of the Mester Advice

Dear IiIr. HalLabrin:
I enclose herewith the california Public Utilities 
commission's
(cPUc's) appeal to the Fair Political Practices Cornmission's
(FPPc's) Advice Letter No. A-90-389, dated July L7, 1990. That
letter concluded that models of a parabolic trough design solar
electric polrer plant collector plate, which were provided to each
nember of the CPUC, are gifts and do not faII under the
'informati,onal materials" exception to the definition of gift
under the Act. The Advice Letter also pointed out in footnote 7,, that because gifts were involved, the 'rule of
necessity,' codified at Government code section 87101., could not
be applied to the facts presented by the CPUC. (In re Hopkins, 3
FPPC ops. 1,07. )
The cPuc's primary arg"ument is that the nodels have been
incorrectly characterized as gifts. 
rurthermore, even if the
FPPC continues to categorize the models as gifts, the FPPC should
1et the cPUc cure the problen by letting the Comrnissioners return
the models irurediately. In additj.on, the FPPC should find that
the holding of the Hopkins opinion on the 'rul-e of necessity"
should not be applied to this set of facts.
The cPUc requests that its appeal be placed on the agenda of the
Fair Political Practices Cornrnission (FPPC) meeting of August 7,
1990, and that the cPUc be given an opportunity to make a
presentation to the FPPC at that tine. 
As explained in the
attached appeal , because of the severe time constraints
which govern the proceeding currently before the CPUC which is
directly affected by the FPPC'S Advice Letter, the CPUC urges the
FPPC to act on this appeal, if at all possible, on August 7.

In your l-etter to the Commission, you sought advice as to whether in itern received by each member of the California Public Utilities Commission (the 'rcPuct') constituted 6 ttgiftrr, or, in the alternative, fe11 under the "informational rnater.ials" exception to the definition of gift under the Act.
